content.text: SyncDocs вҖ“ Real-Time Collaborative Document Editor

content.caption: SyncDocs вҖ” real-time collaborative document editing, built for scale on AWS.

content.text: **SyncDocs** is a production-grade real-time collaborative document editor built for scale. Multiple users can edit the same document simultaneously with changes synced in real time using a **delta-based approach** вҖ” only the diff is transmitted and stored, not the full document on every keystroke. Race conditions between concurrent edits are resolved using an **Operational Transformation (OT) algorithm**.

content.title: Live Platform
content.text: Deployed and live at **sync-docs.vpjoshi.in** вҖ” running on Amazon EKS across 3 availability zones in ap-south-1.
The Problem

Building a truly scalable collaborative editor is hard. Naive implementations send the full document state on every keystroke вҖ” which doesn't scale. Concurrent edits from multiple users create race conditions that corrupt document state. WebSocket connections are stateful and hard to scale horizontally. Write ingestion and processing need to be decoupled to handle traffic spikes without dropping data.

content.title: Core Challenges
content.text: **Race conditions** between concurrent edits В· **Write throughput** at scale В· **Stateful WebSocket** horizontal scaling В· **Shared filesystem** across multi-AZ pods В· **Decoupled processing** without data loss
Solution Overview

items: **Delta-based sync** вҖ” only diffs transmitted and stored, not full document state on every keystroke.
**Operational Transformation (OT)** вҖ” advanced algorithm resolves concurrent edit conflicts and race conditions.
**Kafka (AWS MSK)** вҖ” decouples write ingestion from processing, absorbs traffic spikes without data loss.
**EKS multi-AZ** вҖ” all workloads distributed across 3 AZs with topology spread constraints.
**KEDA autoscaling** вҖ” IO Processor and Analytics scale independently based on Kafka consumer lag.
**EFS shared filesystem** вҖ” ReadWriteMany PVC mounted across all AZ pods, no data locality issues.
**Serverless auth** вҖ” Lambda + DynamoDB + JWT, scales to zero between requests.

content.caption: Multiple users editing the same document simultaneously вҖ” changes synced in real time via delta ops.

content.text: System Architecture

content.caption: Full AWS architecture вҖ” CloudFront вҶ’ EKS вҶ’ MSK вҶ’ IO Processor вҶ’ EFS across 3 AZs.

content.text: Frontend вҖ“ React/Vite on CloudFront + S3

A **static React/Vite SPA** served entirely via CloudFront CDN. Assets are stored in S3 with **Origin Access Control (OAC)** вҖ” the bucket is fully private, only CloudFront can read from it. No origin hits for static content вҖ” everything is cached at the edge.

items: **Vite** вҖ“ Fast build tooling and dev server.
**React** вҖ“ Component-based collaborative editor UI.
**S3 + OAC** вҖ“ Private asset storage, only accessible via CloudFront.
**CloudFront** вҖ“ Global CDN, zero origin hits for static content.
**WebSocket client** вҖ“ Maintains persistent connection to Node.js backend for real-time delta sync.

content.title: OAC vs OAI
content.text: Uses **Origin Access Control (OAC)** вҖ” AWS's modern replacement for the legacy Origin Access Identity (OAI). OAC supports additional S3 operations and is the recommended approach for new CloudFront + S3 deployments.
Serverless Auth вҖ“ Lambda + DynamoDB + JWT

Auth is fully **serverless** вҖ” API Gateway receives auth requests and invokes a Lambda function which validates credentials against DynamoDB and returns a signed JWT. Stateless, no always-on auth server, scales to zero between requests.

content.title: Stateless JWT
content.text: The backend API pods validate JWTs locally вҖ” no calls back to Lambda or DynamoDB per request. The secret is injected via Kubernetes secrets at pod startup.
Backend вҖ“ Node.js on EKS

The **Node.js API server** runs as pods in EKS, handling both REST and WebSocket connections. It acts as the **Kafka producer** вҖ” every document change event (delta op) gets published to MSK topics. WebSocket connections are maintained per-session for real-time bidirectional sync.

content.title: Topology Spread
content.text: Pods use `topologySpreadConstraints` to distribute across all 3 AZs. HPA handles uneven load вҖ” AZ pod counts differ intentionally to reflect real-world asymmetric traffic patterns.
Kafka вҖ“ AWS MSK

**AWS MSK (Managed Streaming for Kafka)** with 3 brokers, one per AZ. Decouples write ingestion from processing вҖ” the backend publishes at any rate and consumers process at their own pace without dropping events.

items: **`doc.delta.ops`** вҖ” document change events (delta ops) from all connected clients.
**`analytics.events`** вҖ” usage events for independent analytics processing.
**Replication factor 3** вҖ” every message replicated across all 3 brokers/AZs for fault tolerance.
**MSK outside EKS** вҖ” sits inside the same VPC/region, accessible to all EKS pods via VPC routing.

content.title: Why Kafka?
content.text: Without Kafka, a traffic spike causes the backend to either drop events or overwhelm the IO Processor. **Kafka absorbs the spike** вҖ” backend publishes freely, IO Processor consumes at its own pace. Zero data loss under load.
IO Processor вҖ“ Delta Consumer

A **Kafka consumer group** subscribed to `doc.delta.ops`. Applies the delta logic вҖ” computes and persists document state changes to EFS. Scales independently of the backend via **KEDA** based on consumer lag.

content.title: KEDA Autoscaling
content.text: **KEDA** watches the `doc.delta.ops` consumer group lag. When lag grows (traffic spike), it scales IO Processor pods up automatically. When lag clears, pods scale back down вҖ” cost-efficient and responsive.
Operational Transformation (OT)

The core algorithm that makes collaborative editing correct. When two users edit the same position simultaneously, their changes arrive out of order at the server. Without OT, one edit silently overwrites the other вҖ” corrupting the document.

content.code:
# The race condition problem:

Document: "Hello"

User A: insert " World" at pos 5  вҶ’ "Hello World"
User B: insert "!" at pos 5       вҶ’ "Hello!"       (concurrent)

# Naive merge (no OT): one op gets dropped or positions collide
# With OT: B's op is transformed against A's op
#   вҶ’ B's insert position adjusted to pos 11
#   вҶ’ Final: "Hello World!"  вң“

content.text: OT transforms each incoming operation against all concurrent operations that have already been applied вҖ” adjusting positions and intent so every user converges to the same document state regardless of network order.

content.title: Delta-Based Storage
content.text: Only **diffs (delta ops)** are transmitted over WebSocket and published to Kafka вҖ” not the full document. This reduces bandwidth by orders of magnitude for large documents with many concurrent editors.
Amazon EFS вҖ“ Shared Filesystem

**Amazon EFS** is mounted across all IO Processor pods in all 3 AZs via the **EFS CSI driver** with a `ReadWriteMany` PVC. Any pod in any AZ can read and write the same document files вҖ” no data locality issues, no pod-to-pod coordination needed.

content.code:
# EFS PVC вҖ” ReadWriteMany across all AZs
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: syncdocs-efs-pvc
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteMany
  storageClassName: efs-sc
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi

content.title: Why not S3?
content.text: S3 is eventually consistent for overwrites вҖ” not suitable for high-frequency document state updates. **EFS gives POSIX filesystem semantics** with strong consistency, making it safe for concurrent IO Processor writes.
Full Technical Stack
Frontend

items: **React + Vite** вҖ“ SPA collaborative editor UI.
**Tailwind CSS** вҖ“ Utility-first styling.
**WebSocket client** вҖ“ Real-time delta sync with backend.
**CloudFront + S3 (OAC)** вҖ“ CDN-served static assets.

content.text: Backend

items: **Node.js** вҖ“ REST + WebSocket API server.
**Kafka producer (MSK)** вҖ“ Publishes delta ops to `doc.delta.ops`.
**JWT validation** вҖ“ Stateless auth on every request.

content.text: Auth

items: **AWS Lambda** вҖ“ Serverless auth handler.
**API Gateway** вҖ“ HTTP trigger for Lambda.
**DynamoDB** вҖ“ User credential storage.
**JWT** вҖ“ Stateless session tokens.

content.text: Messaging & Processing

items: **AWS MSK (Kafka)** вҖ“ 3 brokers, RF=3, two topics.
**IO Processor** вҖ“ Kafka consumer, applies OT, persists to EFS.
**Analytics Processor** вҖ“ Kafka consumer, processes usage metrics.
**KEDA** вҖ“ Autoscales consumers based on Kafka lag.

content.text: Infrastructure

items: **Amazon EKS** вҖ“ Multi-AZ Kubernetes across ap-south-1a/b/c.
**Amazon EFS** вҖ“ ReadWriteMany shared filesystem via CSI driver.
**HPA** вҖ“ Horizontal Pod Autoscaler for backend pods.
**topologySpreadConstraints** вҖ“ Even pod distribution across AZs.

content.text: Scalability Design

items: **Stateless backend** вҖ” JWT auth means any pod can serve any request. HPA scales pods horizontally without session stickiness issues.
**Kafka buffer** вҖ” MSK absorbs write spikes. Backend never blocks waiting for IO Processor.
**KEDA lag-based scaling** вҖ” IO Processor scales precisely to demand, not CPU/RAM proxies.
**Multi-AZ EKS** вҖ” No single AZ is a SPOF. `topologySpreadConstraints` ensures pods survive an AZ outage.
**EFS ReadWriteMany** вҖ” IO Processor pods across all AZs write to shared storage without coordination overhead.
**Delta ops** вҖ” Bandwidth and storage scale with change rate, not document size.

content.title: Zero Single Point of Failure
content.text: Every layer is multi-AZ вҖ” EKS pods, MSK brokers (RF=3), EFS, and DynamoDB. Losing any single AZ keeps SyncDocs fully operational.
